an9.35:1.1.pli: “Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, gāvī pabbateyyā bālā abyattā akhettaññū akusalā visame pabbate carituṁ.
an9.35:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, suppose there was a mountain cow who was foolish, incompetent, unskillful, and lacked common sense when roaming on rugged mountains.

an9.35:1.3.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ agatapubbañceva disaṁ gaccheyyaṁ, akhāditapubbāni ca tiṇāni khādeyyaṁ, apītapubbāni ca pānīyāni piveyyan’ti.
an9.35:1.3.eng: ‘Why don’t I go somewhere I’ve never been before? I could eat grass and drink water that I’ve never tried before.’

an9.35:1.4.pli: Sā purimaṁ pādaṁ na suppatiṭṭhitaṁ patiṭṭhāpetvā pacchimaṁ pādaṁ uddhareyya.
an9.35:1.4.eng: She’d take a step with a fore-hoof; but before it was properly set down, she’d lift up a hind-hoof.

an9.35:1.5.pli: Sā na ceva agatapubbaṁ disaṁ gaccheyya, na ca akhāditapubbāni tiṇāni khādeyya, na ca apītapubbāni pānīyāni piveyya;
an9.35:1.5.eng: She wouldn’t go somewhere she’d never been before, or eat grass and drink water that she’d never tried before.

an9.35:1.6.eng: And she’d never return safely to the place she had started from.

an9.35:1.7.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ agatapubbañceva disaṁ gaccheyyaṁ, akhāditapubbāni ca tiṇāni khādeyyaṁ, apītapubbāni ca pānīyāni piveyyan’ti tañca padesaṁ na sotthinā paccāgaccheyya.

an9.35:1.9.pli: Tathā hi sā, bhikkhave, gāvī pabbateyyā bālā abyattā akhettaññū akusalā visame pabbate carituṁ.
an9.35:1.9.eng: Because that mountain cow was foolish, incompetent, unskillful, and lacked common sense when roaming on rugged mountains.

an9.35:1.10.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, idhekacco bhikkhu bālo abyatto akhettaññū akusalo vivicceva kāmehi vivicca akusalehi dhammehi savitakkaṁ savicāraṁ vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati;
an9.35:1.10.eng: In the same way, some foolish, incompetent, unskillful mendicant, lacking common sense, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected.

an9.35:1.11.pli: so taṁ nimittaṁ na āsevati na bhāveti na bahulīkaroti na svādhiṭṭhitaṁ adhiṭṭhāti.
an9.35:1.11.eng: But they don’t cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis; they don’t ensure it is properly stabilized.

an9.35:2.2.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā ajjhattaṁ sampasādanaṁ cetaso ekodibhāvaṁ avitakkaṁ avicāraṁ samādhijaṁ pītisukhaṁ dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:2.2.eng: ‘Why don’t I, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected.’

an9.35:2.3.pli: So na sakkoti vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā …pe… dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharituṁ.
an9.35:2.3.eng: But they’re not able to enter and remain in the second absorption.

an9.35:2.5.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ vivicceva kāmehi vivicca akusalehi dhammehi savitakkaṁ savicāraṁ vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:2.5.eng: ‘Why don’t I, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected.’

an9.35:2.6.pli: So na sakkoti vivicceva kāmehi …pe… paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharituṁ.
an9.35:2.6.eng: But they’re not able to enter and remain in the first absorption.

an9.35:2.7.pli: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ‘bhikkhu ubhato bhaṭṭho ubhato parihīno, seyyathāpi sā gāvī pabbateyyā bālā abyattā akhettaññū akusalā visame pabbate carituṁ’.
an9.35:2.7.eng: This is called a mendicant who has slipped and fallen from both sides. They’re like the mountain cow who was foolish, incompetent, unskillful, and lacking in common sense when roaming on rugged mountains.

an9.35:3.1.pli: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, gāvī pabbateyyā paṇḍitā byattā khettaññū kusalā visame pabbate carituṁ.
an9.35:3.1.eng: Suppose there was a mountain cow who was astute, competent, skillful, and used common sense when roaming on rugged mountains.

an9.35:3.3.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ agatapubbañceva disaṁ gaccheyyaṁ, akhāditapubbāni ca tiṇāni khādeyyaṁ, apītapubbāni ca pānīyāni piveyyan’ti.
an9.35:3.3.eng: ‘Why don’t I go somewhere I’ve never been before? I could eat grass and drink water that I’ve never tried before.’

an9.35:3.4.pli: Sā purimaṁ pādaṁ suppatiṭṭhitaṁ patiṭṭhāpetvā pacchimaṁ pādaṁ uddhareyya.
an9.35:3.4.eng: She’d take a step with a fore-hoof; and after it was properly set down, she’d lift up a hind-hoof.

an9.35:3.5.pli: Sā agatapubbañceva disaṁ gaccheyya, akhāditapubbāni ca tiṇāni khādeyya, apītapubbāni ca pānīyāni piveyya.
an9.35:3.5.eng: She’d go somewhere she’d never been before, and eat grass and drink water that she’d never tried before.

an9.35:3.6.eng: And she’d return safely to the place she had started from.

an9.35:3.7.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ agatapubbañceva disaṁ gaccheyyaṁ, akhāditapubbāni ca tiṇāni khādeyyaṁ, apītapubbāni ca pānīyāni piveyyan’ti tañca padesaṁ sotthinā paccāgaccheyya.

an9.35:3.9.pli: Tathā hi sā, bhikkhave, gāvī pabbateyyā paṇḍitā byattā khettaññū kusalā visame pabbate carituṁ.
an9.35:3.9.eng: Because that mountain cow was astute, competent, skillful, and used common sense when roaming on rugged mountains.

an9.35:3.10.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, idhekacco bhikkhu paṇḍito byatto khettaññū kusalo vivicceva kāmehi vivicca akusalehi dhammehi savitakkaṁ savicāraṁ vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.35:3.10.eng: In the same way, some astute, competent, skillful mendicant, using common sense, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected.

an9.35:3.11.pli: So taṁ nimittaṁ āsevati bhāveti bahulīkaroti svādhiṭṭhitaṁ adhiṭṭhāti.
an9.35:3.11.eng: They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized.

an9.35:4.2.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā ajjhattaṁ sampasādanaṁ cetaso ekodibhāvaṁ avitakkaṁ avicāraṁ samādhijaṁ pītisukhaṁ dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:4.2.eng: ‘Why don’t I, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected.’

an9.35:4.3.pli: So dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ anabhihiṁsamāno vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā … dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.35:4.3.eng: Without charging at the second absorption, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption.

an9.35:4.4.pli: So taṁ nimittaṁ āsevati bhāveti bahulīkaroti svādhiṭṭhitaṁ adhiṭṭhāti.
an9.35:4.4.eng: They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized.

an9.35:5.2.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ pītiyā ca virāgā upekkhako ca vihareyyaṁ sato ca sampajāno, sukhañca kāyena paṭisaṁvedeyyaṁ yaṁ taṁ ariyā ācikkhanti—upekkhako satimā sukhavihārīti tatiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:5.2.eng: ‘Why don’t I, with the fading away of rapture, enter and remain in the third absorption, where I will meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, “Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.”’

an9.35:5.3.pli: So tatiyaṁ jhānaṁ anabhihiṁsamāno pītiyā ca virāgā … tatiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.35:5.3.eng: Without charging at the third absorption, with the fading away of rapture, they enter and remain in the third absorption.

an9.35:5.4.pli: So taṁ nimittaṁ āsevati bhāveti bahulīkaroti svādhiṭṭhitaṁ adhiṭṭhāti.
an9.35:5.4.eng: They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized.

an9.35:6.2.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ sukhassa ca pahānā dukkhassa ca pahānā pubbeva somanassadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamā adukkhamasukhaṁ upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:6.2.eng: ‘Why don’t I, with the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness.’

an9.35:6.3.pli: So catutthaṁ jhānaṁ anabhihiṁsamāno sukhassa ca pahānā …pe… catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.35:6.3.eng: Without charging at the fourth absorption, with the giving up of pleasure and pain, and the ending of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption.

an9.35:6.4.pli: So taṁ nimittaṁ āsevati bhāveti bahulīkaroti svādhiṭṭhitaṁ adhiṭṭhāti.
an9.35:6.4.eng: They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized.

an9.35:7.2.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ sabbaso rūpasaññānaṁ samatikkamā paṭighasaññānaṁ atthaṅgamā nānattasaññānaṁ amanasikārā ananto ākāsoti ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:7.2.eng: ‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that “space is infinite”, enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space.’

an9.35:7.3.pli: So ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ anabhihiṁsamāno sabbaso rūpasaññānaṁ samatikkamā …pe… ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.35:7.3.eng: Without charging at the dimension of infinite space, with the fading away of rapture, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite space.

an9.35:7.4.pli: So taṁ nimittaṁ āsevati bhāveti bahulīkaroti svādhiṭṭhitaṁ adhiṭṭhāti.
an9.35:7.4.eng: They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized.

an9.35:8.2.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ sabbaso ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma anantaṁ viññāṇanti viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:8.2.eng: ‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite space, aware that “consciousness is infinite”, enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness.’

an9.35:8.3.pli: So viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ anabhihiṁsamāno sabbaso ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘anantaṁ viññāṇan’ti viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.35:8.3.eng: Without charging at the dimension of infinite consciousness, they enter and remain in the dimension of infinite consciousness.

an9.35:8.4.pli: So taṁ nimittaṁ āsevati bhāveti bahulīkaroti svādhiṭṭhitaṁ adhiṭṭhāti.
an9.35:8.4.eng: They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized.

an9.35:9.2.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ sabbaso viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma natthi kiñcīti ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:9.2.eng: ‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that “there is nothing at all”, enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness.’

an9.35:9.3.pli: So ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ anabhihiṁsamāno sabbaso viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘natthi kiñcī’ti ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.35:9.3.eng: Without charging at the dimension of nothingness, they enter and remain in the dimension of nothingness.

an9.35:9.4.pli: So taṁ nimittaṁ āsevati bhāveti bahulīkaroti svādhiṭṭhitaṁ adhiṭṭhāti.
an9.35:9.4.eng: They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized.

an9.35:10.2.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ sabbaso ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ samatikkamma nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:10.2.eng: ‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of nothingness, enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.’

an9.35:10.3.pli: So nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ anabhihiṁsamāno sabbaso ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ samatikkamma nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.35:10.3.eng: Without charging at the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, they enter and remain in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.

an9.35:10.4.pli: So taṁ nimittaṁ āsevati bhāveti bahulīkaroti svādhiṭṭhitaṁ adhiṭṭhāti.
an9.35:10.4.eng: They cultivate, develop, and make much of that basis, ensuring that it’s properly stabilized.

an9.35:11.2.pli: ‘yannūnāhaṁ sabbaso nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ samatikkamma saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ upasampajja vihareyyan’ti.
an9.35:11.2.eng: ‘Why don’t I, going totally beyond the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling.’

an9.35:11.3.pli: So saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ anabhihiṁsamāno sabbaso nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ samatikkamma saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.35:11.3.eng: Without charging at the cessation of perception and feeling, they enter and remain in the cessation of perception and feeling.

an9.35:12.1.pli: Yato kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu taṁ tadeva samāpattiṁ samāpajjatipi vuṭṭhātipi, tassa mudu cittaṁ hoti kammaññaṁ.
an9.35:12.1.eng: When a mendicant enters and emerges from all these attainments, their mind becomes pliable and workable.

an9.35:12.2.pli: Mudunā kammaññena cittena appamāṇo samādhi hoti subhāvito.
an9.35:12.2.eng: With a pliable and workable mind, their immersion becomes limitless and well developed.

an9.35:12.3.pli: So appamāṇena samādhinā subhāvitena yassa yassa abhiññāsacchikaraṇīyassa dhammassa cittaṁ abhininnāmeti abhiññāsacchikiriyāya tatra tatreva sakkhibhabbataṁ pāpuṇāti sati sati āyatane.
an9.35:12.3.eng: They extend the mind to realize by insight each and every thing that can be realized by insight; and they are capable of realizing those things, since each and every one is within range.

an9.35:13.1.pli: So sace ākaṅkhati: ‘anekavihitaṁ iddhividhaṁ paccanubhaveyyaṁ—ekopi hutvā bahudhā assaṁ, bahudhāpi hutvā eko assaṁ …pe… yāva brahmalokāpi kāyena vasaṁ vatteyyan’ti,
an9.35:13.1.eng: They might wish: ‘May I wield the many kinds of psychic power: multiplying myself and becoming one again … controlling my body as far as the realm of divinity.’

an9.35:13.2.pli: tatra tatreva sakkhibhabbataṁ pāpuṇāti sati sati āyatane.
an9.35:13.2.eng: They are capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.

an9.35:14.1.eng: They might wish: ‘With clairaudience that is purified and superhuman, may I hear both kinds of sounds, human and heavenly, whether near or far.’

an9.35:14.2.eng: They are capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.

an9.35:15.1.pli: So sace ākaṅkhati: ‘parasattānaṁ parapuggalānaṁ cetasā ceto paricca pajāneyyaṁ,
an9.35:15.1.eng: They might wish: ‘May I understand the minds of other beings and individuals, having encompassed them with my mind.

an9.35:15.2.pli: sarāgaṁ vā cittaṁ sarāgaṁ cittanti pajāneyyaṁ,
an9.35:15.2.eng: May I understand mind with greed as “mind with greed”,

an9.35:15.3.pli: vītarāgaṁ vā cittaṁ vītarāgaṁ cittanti pajāneyyaṁ,
an9.35:15.3.eng: and mind without greed as “mind without greed”;

an9.35:15.4.pli: sadosaṁ vā cittaṁ sadosaṁ cittanti pajāneyyaṁ,
an9.35:15.4.eng: mind with hate as “mind with hate”,

an9.35:15.5.pli: vītadosaṁ vā cittaṁ vītadosaṁ cittanti pajāneyyaṁ,
an9.35:15.5.eng: and mind without hate as “mind without hate”;

an9.35:15.6.pli: samohaṁ vā cittaṁ samohaṁ cittanti pajāneyyaṁ,
an9.35:15.6.eng: mind with delusion as “mind with delusion”,

an9.35:15.7.eng: and mind without delusion as “mind without delusion”;

an9.35:15.17.pli: avimuttaṁ vā cittaṁ avimuttaṁ cittanti pajāneyyan’ti,

an9.35:15.18.pli: tatra tatreva sakkhibhabbataṁ pāpuṇāti sati sati āyatane.
an9.35:15.18.eng: They are capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.

an9.35:16.1.pli: So sace ākaṅkhati: ‘anekavihitaṁ pubbenivāsaṁ anussareyyaṁ, seyyathidaṁ—ekampi jātiṁ dvepi jātiyo …pe… iti sākāraṁ sauddesaṁ anekavihitaṁ pubbenivāsaṁ anussareyyan’ti,
an9.35:16.1.eng: They might wish: ‘May I recollect many kinds of past lives. That is: one, two, three, four, five, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand rebirths; many eons of the world contracting, many eons of the world expanding, many eons of the world contracting and expanding. They remember: “There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn somewhere else. There, too, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended. When I passed away from that place I was reborn here.” May I recollect my many past lives, with features and details.’

an9.35:16.2.pli: tatra tatreva sakkhibhabbataṁ pāpuṇāti sati sati āyatane.
an9.35:16.2.eng: They’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.

an9.35:17.1.pli: So sace ākaṅkhati: ‘dibbena cakkhunā visuddhena atikkantamānusakena …pe… yathākammūpage satte pajāneyyan’ti
an9.35:17.1.eng: They might wish: ‘With clairvoyance that is purified and superhuman, may I see sentient beings passing away and being reborn—inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good place or a bad place—and understand how sentient beings pass on according to their deeds.’

an9.35:17.2.pli: tatra tatreva sakkhibhabbataṁ pāpuṇāti sati sati āyatane.
an9.35:17.2.eng: They’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.

an9.35:18.1.pli: So sace ākaṅkhati: ‘āsavānaṁ khayā anāsavaṁ cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ diṭṭheva dhamme sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja vihareyyan’ti,
an9.35:18.1.eng: They might wish: ‘May I realize the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life, and live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.’

an9.35:18.2.pli: tatra tatreva sakkhibhabbataṁ pāpuṇāti sati sati āyatane”ti.
an9.35:18.2.eng: They’re capable of realizing it, since each and every one is within range.”

an9.36:1.1.pli: “Paṭhamampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
an9.36:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, I say that the first absorption is a basis for ending the defilements.

an9.36:1.2.pli: dutiyampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
an9.36:1.2.eng: The second absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.

an9.36:1.3.pli: tatiyampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
an9.36:1.3.eng: The third absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.

an9.36:1.4.pli: catutthampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
an9.36:1.4.eng: The fourth absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.

an9.36:1.5.pli: ākāsānañcāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
an9.36:1.5.eng: The dimension of infinite space is also a basis for ending the defilements.

an9.36:1.6.pli: viññāṇañcāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
an9.36:1.6.eng: The dimension of infinite consciousness is also a basis for ending the defilements.

an9.36:1.7.pli: ākiñcaññāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
an9.36:1.7.eng: The dimension of nothingness is also a basis for ending the defilements.

an9.36:1.8.pli: nevasaññānāsaññāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
an9.36:1.8.eng: The dimension of neither perception nor non-perception is also a basis for ending the defilements.

an9.36:1.9.pli: saññāvedayitanirodhampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi.
an9.36:1.9.eng: The cessation of perception and feeling is also a basis for ending the defilements.

an9.36:2.1.pli: ‘Paṭhamampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
an9.36:2.1.eng: ‘The first absorption is a basis for ending the defilements.’

an9.36:2.2.eng: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?

an9.36:2.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vivicceva kāmehi …pe… paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.36:2.3.eng: Take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption.

an9.36:2.4.pli: So yadeva tattha hoti rūpagataṁ vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
an9.36:2.4.eng: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.

an9.36:2.5.eng: They turn their mind away from those things,

an9.36:2.6.pli: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
an9.36:2.6.eng: and apply it to freedom from death:

an9.36:2.7.pli: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
an9.36:2.7.eng: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’

an9.36:2.8.pli: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
an9.36:2.8.eng: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.

an9.36:2.9.pli: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
an9.36:2.9.eng: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.

an9.36:3.1.pli: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, issāso vā issāsantevāsī vā tiṇapurisarūpake vā mattikāpuñje vā yoggaṁ karitvā, so aparena samayena dūrepātī ca hoti akkhaṇavedhī ca mahato ca kāyassa padāletā;
an9.36:3.1.eng: It’s like an archer or their apprentice who first practices on a straw man or a clay model. At a later time they become a long-distance shooter, a marksman, who shatters large objects.

an9.36:3.2.pli: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vivicceva kāmehi …pe… paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.36:3.2.eng: In the same way a noble disciple, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, enters and remains in the first absorption.

an9.36:3.3.pli: So yadeva tattha hoti rūpagataṁ vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
an9.36:3.3.eng: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.

an9.36:3.4.eng: They turn their mind away from those things,

an9.36:3.5.pli: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
an9.36:3.5.eng: and apply it to freedom from death:

an9.36:3.6.pli: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
an9.36:3.6.eng: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’

an9.36:3.7.pli: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
an9.36:3.7.eng: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.

an9.36:3.8.pli: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
an9.36:3.8.eng: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.

an9.36:3.9.pli: ‘Paṭhamampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti,
an9.36:3.9.eng: ‘The first absorption is a basis for ending the defilements.’

an9.36:3.10.pli: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ, idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
an9.36:3.10.eng: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.

an9.36:4.1.eng: ‘The second absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ …

an9.36:4.2.eng: ‘The third absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ …

an9.36:4.3.pli: ‘catutthampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
an9.36:4.3.eng: ‘The fourth absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ …

an9.36:4.5.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sukhassa ca pahānā dukkhassa ca pahānā pubbeva somanassadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamā adukkhamasukhaṁ upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.

an9.36:4.6.pli: So yadeva tattha hoti rūpagataṁ vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.

an9.36:4.8.pli: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:

an9.36:4.9.pli: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.

an9.36:4.10.pli: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.

an9.36:4.11.pli: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.

an9.36:5.1.pli: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, issāso vā issāsantevāsī vā tiṇapurisarūpake vā mattikāpuñje vā yoggaṁ karitvā, so aparena samayena dūrepātī ca hoti akkhaṇavedhī ca mahato ca kāyassa padāletā;

an9.36:5.2.pli: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sukhassa ca pahānā …pe… catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.

an9.36:5.3.pli: So yadeva tattha hoti rūpagataṁ vedanāgataṁ …pe…

an9.36:5.5.pli: ‘Catutthampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ, idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.

an9.36:6.1.pli: ‘Ākāsānañcāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
an9.36:6.1.eng: ‘The dimension of infinite space is also a basis for ending the defilements.’

an9.36:6.2.eng: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?

an9.36:6.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso rūpasaññānaṁ samatikkamā paṭighasaññānaṁ atthaṅgamā nānattasaññānaṁ amanasikārā ‘ananto ākāso’ti ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.36:6.3.eng: Take a mendicant who, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space.

an9.36:6.4.pli: So yadeva tattha hoti vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
an9.36:6.4.eng: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.

an9.36:6.5.eng: They turn their mind away from those things,

an9.36:6.6.pli: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
an9.36:6.6.eng: and apply it to freedom from death:

an9.36:6.7.pli: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
an9.36:6.7.eng: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’

an9.36:6.8.pli: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
an9.36:6.8.eng: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.

an9.36:6.9.pli: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
an9.36:6.9.eng: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.

an9.36:7.1.pli: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, issāso vā issāsantevāsī vā tiṇapurisarūpake vā mattikāpuñje vā yoggaṁ karitvā, so aparena samayena dūrepātī ca hoti akkhaṇavedhī ca mahato ca kāyassa padāletā;
an9.36:7.1.eng: It’s like an archer or their apprentice who first practices on a straw man or a clay model. At a later time they become a long-distance shooter, a marksman, who shatters large objects.

an9.36:7.2.pli: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso rūpasaññānaṁ samatikkamā paṭighasaññānaṁ atthaṅgamā nānattasaññānaṁ amanasikārā ‘ananto ākāso’ti ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.36:7.2.eng: In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. …

an9.36:7.3.pli: So yadeva tattha hoti vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ …pe…

an9.36:7.5.eng: ‘The dimension of infinite space is a basis for ending the defilements.’

an9.36:7.6.pli: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ, idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
an9.36:7.6.eng: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.

an9.36:8.1.eng: ‘The dimension of infinite consciousness is a basis for ending the defilements.’ …

an9.36:8.2.pli: ākiñcaññāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
an9.36:8.2.eng: ‘The dimension of nothingness is a basis for ending the defilements.’

an9.36:8.3.eng: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?

an9.36:8.4.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘natthi kiñcī’ti ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.36:8.4.eng: Take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness.

an9.36:8.5.pli: So yadeva tattha hoti vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
an9.36:8.5.eng: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.

an9.36:8.6.eng: They turn their mind away from those things,

an9.36:8.7.pli: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
an9.36:8.7.eng: and apply it to freedom from death:

an9.36:8.8.pli: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
an9.36:8.8.eng: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’

an9.36:8.9.pli: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
an9.36:8.9.eng: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.

an9.36:8.10.pli: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
an9.36:8.10.eng: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.

an9.36:9.1.pli: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, issāso vā issāsantevāsī vā tiṇapurisarūpake vā mattikāpuñje vā yoggaṁ karitvā, so aparena samayena dūrepātī ca hoti akkhaṇavedhī ca mahato ca kāyassa padāletā;
an9.36:9.1.eng: It’s like an archer or their apprentice who first practices on a straw man or a clay model. At a later time they become a long-distance shooter, a marksman, who shatters large objects.

an9.36:9.2.pli: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘natthi kiñcī’ti ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
an9.36:9.2.eng: In the same way, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness.

an9.36:9.3.pli: So yadeva tattha hoti vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
an9.36:9.3.eng: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.

an9.36:9.4.eng: They turn their mind away from those things,

an9.36:9.5.pli: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
an9.36:9.5.eng: and apply it to freedom from death:

an9.36:9.6.pli: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
an9.36:9.6.eng: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’

an9.36:9.7.pli: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
an9.36:9.7.eng: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.

an9.36:9.8.pli: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
an9.36:9.8.eng: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.

an9.36:9.9.eng: ‘The dimension of nothingness is a basis for ending the defilements.’

an9.36:9.10.pli: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ, idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
an9.36:9.10.eng: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.

an9.36:10.1.pli: Iti kho, bhikkhave, yāvatā saññāsamāpatti tāvatā aññāpaṭivedho.
an9.36:10.1.eng: And so, mendicants, penetration to enlightenment extends as far as attainments with perception.

an9.36:10.2.pli: Yāni ca kho imāni, bhikkhave, nissāya dve āyatanāni—
an9.36:10.2.eng: But the two dimensions that depend on these—

an9.36:10.3.pli: nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasamāpatti ca saññāvedayitanirodho ca, jhāyīhete, bhikkhave, samāpattikusalehi samāpattivuṭṭhānakusalehi samāpajjitvā vuṭṭhahitvā sammā akkhātabbānīti vadāmī”ti.
an9.36:10.3.eng: the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, and the cessation of perception and feeling—are properly explained by meditators who are skilled in these attainments and skilled in emerging from them, after they’ve entered them and emerged from them.”
